petitioner_background: The petitioner is a project management specialist from Kazakhstan (inferred from the Letter of Recognition issued by a Kazakhstan city mayor) who sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under EB-1A, INA section 203(b)(1)(A). His claimed qualifications included overseeing the safety and security of a hospital, for which he received recognition from a local mayor. Before the Director, he claimed to meet three of the regulatory criteria, and on appeal he maintained the same evidentiary claims.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner provided a Letter of Recognition from a Kazakhstan city mayor for his services overseeing the safety and security of a hospital. On appeal, he argued that because the mayor of a large city issued the letter, this represented the letter's significance, and he noted other correspondence in the record 'underscores the substantial impact of [the Petitioner's] leadership,' supporting his position that the letter qualified under this criterion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the Director that the evidence did not satisfy this criterion. The AAO explained that national and international recognition results not from the individual who signed the prize or award, but through awareness of the accolade in the eyes of the field nationally or internationally, evident through means such as national or international-level media coverage. The AAO analogized to Presidential Letters of Appreciation signed for retiring military/DoD civilian personnel and the Presidential Physical Fitness Award, noting that the fact that someone in a position of authority signs a document does not transform it into a nationally or internationally recognized item. The AAO found that the fact the Letter of Recognition was issued by a local government official does not prove that the correspondence is nationally recognized, the Petitioner provided no evidence the mayoral letter was publicly awarded or reported on, and the record did not show that any recognition went beyond the two involved parties. The AAO also noted the Petitioner did not demonstrate the accolade was issued for excellence in his field.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the Petitioner failed to satisfy the awards criterion and therefore could not numerically meet the required three of ten regulatory criteria under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). The AAO reserved the Petitioner's remaining claims under the original contributions criterion (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(v)) and leading or critical role criterion (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(viii)) because it was unnecessary to reach them given the numerical failure. Although it declined to conduct a formal Kazarian final merits determination, the AAO advised that it reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded the record did not support a finding that the Petitioner established the acclaim and recognition required for the classification sought.
legal_citations: Matter of Christo's Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537, 537 n.2 (AAO 2015)
Matter of Larios-Gutierrez De Pablo & Pablo-Larios, 28 I&N Dec. 868 (BIA 2024)
